old_claim.note: Public Museum Catalogs provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Public museum exhibition practice supplied the display context for Patent Office model galleries, but the patent model collection remained a specialized invention archive rather than an art museum.

source_shape.source_locator: USPTO says the 1840 Patent Office was the first federally funded museum collection in U.S. history and displayed rows of patent models to visitors.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source frames the Patent Office galleries as a public museum collection, not a printed catalog tradition.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports public_art_museums as shared exhibition context and weakens the relationship because the collection was a patent-office invention archive rather than a direct descendant of printed cabinet catalogs.

ontology_before: Patent Model Collections depended on Printed Cabinet Catalogs through a generic enabling edge.
ontology_after: Patent Model Collections uses public_art_museums as a contextual public-exhibition precedent and no longer depends on printed cabinet catalogs.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: patent_model_collections now depends on public_art_museums as common_dependency and no longer directly on public_museum_catalogs.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to printed catalogs of invention models rather than the Patent Office model collection.
A source showed printed cabinet catalogs were a direct prerequisite for Patent Office galleries.
The public_art_museums node were narrowed to exclude public collecting and exhibiting institutions.
short_reason: Patent model galleries are museum-display context; printed cabinet catalogs are too narrow and indirect.
